name: Statutory dues payable to government
intent: Ledgers representing tax or statutory amounts deducted, collected, or provided for payment to the government or a statutory authority — not a person or business the company contracts with. The 'payee' behind this ledger is the government itself, so TDS can never apply to amounts credited here. Covers TDS/TCS payable, provident fund (PF/EPF/VPF) payable, ESI/ESIC payable, labour welfare fund payable, professional tax payable, GST payable (including reverse-charge/RCM payable), advance tax, and income-tax payable ledgers.

name: Provision and accrual aggregate ledgers
intent: Internal year-end accounting buckets that accrue expenses before the actual payee is known or billed — provision for expenses, provision for audit fees, outstanding/accrued expenses, and aggregate 'expenses payable' ledgers. These have no single external payee; the real party ledger is credited later, in the settlement voucher, and gets selected on its own. Only aggregate accounting buckets belong here — a ledger named after a specific person or business is NEVER this category.

name: Tax-recoverable, prepaid, and asset-adjustment ledgers
intent: Balance-sheet adjustment ledgers on the asset side that get credited during expense bookings (amortisation, input-credit adjustment) but represent no payee: GST/CGST/SGST/IGST recoverable, input tax credit (ITC), TDS/TCS receivable, and prepaid expense ledgers (prepaid insurance, prepaid rent, prepaid AMC). Crediting these ledgers moves value between the company's own balance-sheet buckets — no external party is being paid.

name: Internal suspense, rounding, and clearing ledgers
intent: Internal offset ledgers used to balance entries or park unresolved differences — suspense accounts, round-off, short & excess, exchange-difference, payroll clearing, and inter-branch/inter-company clearing accounts. No external party exists behind these ledgers.
